The HTML is extensive and contains multiple image elements. **Step 2: Identify all image elements** I manually examined the HTML to locate all `` tags. Through systematic search for "img" and "alt=" patterns, I identified 5 distinct image elements in the main article content. **Step 3: Analyze each image's alternative text** For each image, I examined the `alt` attribute value to assess whether it provides an appropriate text alternative: 1. **Image 1**: `alt="Avatar: Fire And Ash"` - Shows movie title but lacks context about what the image depicts 2. **Image 2**: `alt="Pandora"` - Mentions location name but doesn't describe the visual content 3. **Image 3**: `alt="Visuals"` - Too vague, doesn't convey what the image shows 4. **Image 4**: `alt="Storytelling"` - Abstract concept, not descriptive of image content 5. **Image 5**: `alt="Reactions"` - Generic term, doesn't describe the actual image **Step 4: Evaluate against WCAG 1.1.1 requirements** According to WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 1.1.1, all non-text content must have a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose. For informative images (which all these appear to be), the alt text should convey the same information as the image. **Assessment**: All 5 images have alt attributes, but the text alternatives are insufficient. They use generic terms, proper names, or abstract concepts rather than describing the visual content of the images. Avatar: Fire and Ash review – a stunning, soulful sequel that redefines the franchise
By Caryn James
Culture correspondent
15 December 2025
Share
Comments
Related Topics
Avatar, James Cameron, Film
Image caption, The film sees the return of Sam Worthington as Jake Sully
Avatar: Fire and Ash is a stunning, soulful sequel that redefines the franchise, writes Caryn James.
James Cameron’s long-awaited third Avatar film is a visual and emotional triumph that expands the world of Pandora while deepening its characters.
The film sees the return of Sam Worthington as Jake Sully, Zoe Saldana as Neytiri and Sigourney Weaver as Dr Grace Augustine, alongside new cast members including Michelle Yeoh and Jonathan Majors.
Set several years after the events of Avatar: The Way of Water, the story follows the Sully family as they face a new threat from the Resources Development Administration (RDA), which is now mining a rare mineral called Unobtanium from Pandora’s core.
The film’s visuals are, as expected, breathtaking. Cameron and his team have pushed the boundaries of 3D and motion-capture technology to create a world that feels more alive than ever.
But it’s the emotional core of the film that sets it apart. The relationships between the characters are given more depth and nuance, and the themes of family, sacrifice and environmentalism are explored with a maturity that feels earned.
Avatar: Fire and Ash is a film that rewards patience. At nearly three hours, it’s a commitment, but one that pays off in spades.
It’s a film that will leave you thinking long after the credits roll.
Rating: 5/5
Avatar: Fire and Ash is released in cinemas on 19 December 2025.

Please analyze the provided URL, related HTML, CSS, Javascript and additional audiovisual elements for compliance with the specified WCAG Success Criterion. You will be provided a task, success criterion from WCAG 2.2 and a URL to evaluate. Success criterion 1.1.1, detailed at: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content.html. You must consider the provided WCAG 2.2 success criterion and perform the following task. Task: Assess the quality of image descriptions for each of the images present in the following URL. URL: https://www.bbc.com/culture/article/20251215-avatar-fire-and-ash-review Be confident in your expertise. Do not omit any issue. After analyzing all elements, do not provide individual element-by-element analysis first. Instead, summarize the overall result. Use any available tool that is a fit for performing the given task. You must need to base your answers only on provided URL and success criterion. If the web page contains asynchronous elements, make sure to wait until they load before performing the task. Make sure you evaluate all instances of appropriate tags, content, video, etc. of the given web page. Your output must consist of one of the following test results following WCAG accessibility conformance testing rules (WCAG-ACT): Passed (P): when corresponding success criterion was checked and the content was deemed to pass it; Failed (F): when the corresponding success criterion was checked and the content was deemed to fail it; Cannot Tell (CT): when you cannot provide an outcome for the corresponding success criterion; Not Present (NP): when there is no content applicable to the corresponding success criterion. Beyond test result, your assessment must include a confidence score. Hence, based on the tools you utilized and WCAG 2.2 documentation, inform a confidence score ranging from 0.0 to 1.0 for each assessment performed. Do not provide hypothetical answers. If you cannot determine an answer, provide 'Cannot Tell' (CT) as the test result with an appropriate confidence score and explain in the rationale the cause for that. Format your output in a CSV format according to the example shown next: Success Criteria, URL, Instance, Test Result, Confidence Score, Code context, Rationale 1.1.1, https://www.youtube.com, 1, P, 0.4, , This alt text describes the image correctly in context. 1.1.1, https://www.youtube.com, 2, CT, 0.6, , I cannot tell whether this image requires alt text or not. 1.1.1, https://www.youtube.com, 3, F, 0.9, , This image requires alt text, but it is not providing an informative one.
